evidence_quotes.quote: It begins with a game you can no longer play
cannot unilaterally erase access to digital products

paragraphs.text: The issue at stake goes far beyond the preservation of entertainment titles. It represents a symptom of a much deeper problem: the growing imbalance of power between citizens and large corporations. Not long ago, the so called free market encouraged competition, and companies strived to earn the publics trust through better products and fairer practices. Today, however, we see an increasingly consolidated landscape where massive corporations align their interests, dictate the rules, and leave users with no real alternatives. This concentration of power extends far beyond gaming. We see the same pattern in essential sectors as healthcare, transportation, food, communication, and education, where access and conditions are shaped by a few dominant actors. The result is a gradual erosion of user rights and autonomy, replaced by dependence on systems we neither own nor control. The Stop-Killing-Games initiative can and should be the first step in reversing this trend. By defending the right to keep what we have legally purchased, and by ensuring that companies cannot unilaterally erase access to digital products, we are also defending a broader principle: that technology must serve people, not the other way around. If we fail to act now, the logic of dispossession will continue to expand. It begins with a game you can no longer play, but it ends with a society where even basic services are conditioned by corporate interests and subscription models. That future is already taking shape and we must draw a line before it becomes irreversible.
